figures.caption: Figure 5: Qualitative visual comparison between Any3DAvatar and other baselines.
Figure 9: Examples of AI-generated heads used in our dataset construction pipeline.
Figure 14: Visual comparison with non-full-head 3D head reconstruction methods.
Table 1: Quantitative comparison on the AnyHead dataset.
Table 2: Quantitative comparison on the NeRSemble dataset.
Figure 13: Visual comparison with non-full-head 3D head generation methods.
Figure 2: Overview of our AnyHead dataset, which consists of three complementary subsets: AI-Generated Heads, Digital Human Heads, and Accessory-Rich Heads.
Figure 12: Visual comparison with image-to-3D methods.
Figure 6: Quantitative comparison of training with and without auxiliary view-conditioned appearance supervision (AVAS) on different viewpoint angles (image index).

Figure 3: Pipeline of Any3DAvatar. Given a single portrait, we extract image and Gaussian tokens, jointly denoise them with a DiT backbone, and decode the outputs into 3D Gaussian point clouds through a single-step feed-forward path at inference. During training, we additionally supervise the same tokens with a view-conditioned image decoding branch and a multi-task objective that combines reconstruction and perceptual losses.
Figure 17: We evaluate our method on more stylized heads, including complex lighting, non-frontal viewpoints, anime-style heads, and 3D-stylized heads, demonstrating strong generation quality.
Figure 11: Examples of accessory-rich heads generated by controlled multi-view accessory augmentation.
Figure 10: Examples of digital human heads rendered in UE MetaHuman with diverse appearance attributes and expressions.
Figure 8: Visual results of AVAS ablation study. In non-input viewpoints, training with AVAS better preserves texture details in non-input views.
Figure 4: Multi-View renderings of Gaussian point clouds obtained with different denoising step counts. We observe that the quality improvement from multi-step denoising is marginal.
Figure 15: Visualized accessory rendering results. Our method provides diverse accessory generation capabilities.
Figure 16: Impact of different noise injection strategies on our experimental results.
